findings.evidence_quote:
File for $0 on the app
Switch to TurboTax, do it yourself and file for free on the app by 2/18. See offer details
findings.location: hero section 'File for $0 on the app' promo
findings.reasoning: Trigger: the page repeatedly mentions filing 'for $0'/'free' via the TurboTax app, which is a mention of TurboTax's free filing product, triggering R-01. Requirement: the rule requires that directly underneath any free-product mention, the eligibility disclosure '~37% of filers qualify. Simple Form 1040 returns only (no schedules, except for EITC, CTC, student loan interest, and Schedule 1-A)' appear. Here, immediately below the '$0 on the app' headline there is only a link 'See offer details' and bullet points about the mobile-app-only offer (must not have filed with TurboTax last year, must use app, DIY only, deadline 2/18) — none of which state the ~37% qualification statistic or the simple-Form-1040/no-schedules limitation. This is a different offer disclosure (the app promotional offer) but it still constitutes a 'TurboTax free' mention that lacks the required standard free-eligibility disclosure. Counter-reading: one could argue this is a promotional app-specific offer ('file for free on the app') distinct from 'TurboTax Free Edition,' and thus the standard Free Edition disclosure may not apply since eligibility here is offer-based (must not have filed last year), not based on simple-1040 forms. However, the rule states broadly 'If TurboTax free is mentioned' without carving out promotional offers, and the free-filing capability is still being marketed to consumers, so the safest, rule-literal reading is that the disclosure was required and is absent. Given the literal breadth of the rule text and absence of the specific disclosure, axis A is not compliant. Axis B: since no matching disclosure text appears at all, axis B is false (not approved wording used). This yields unapproved_violation and a flag.

findings.evidence_quote: Switch to TurboTax, do it yourself by 2/18 to file for free on the app.
findings.location: 'All tax forms. $0 in the app.' section
findings.reasoning: Trigger: this section again mentions filing 'for free' via the app, a free-product mention, triggering R-01. Requirement: the required ~37%-qualify / simple-1040 disclosure must appear directly underneath. Instead, what follows are four bullet points describing offer eligibility (must not have filed with TurboTax last year, must use the mobile app, DIY product only, must file by Feb 18, 2025) — an entirely different set of conditions unrelated to the required Free Edition tax-form eligibility disclosure. The strongest counter-reading is that this 'file for free' offer refers to a limited promotional deal for switchers using the app, which naturally carries its own bespoke terms rather than the standard Free Edition eligibility, and IRS-form-based scope may not apply the same way to a promotional freebie. Yet the rule's trigger is worded generally ('if TurboTax free is mentioned'), and does not exempt promotional offers, so under strict compliance reading, the omission of the required stat/disclaimer is a violation. Axis B fails since none of the approved disclosure text appears verbatim or in substance. This produces an unapproved_violation and flag verdict, though flagged with moderate confidence given the offer-vs-Free-Edition ambiguity.

findings.evidence_quote:
File for $0 on the app
Switch to TurboTax, do it yourself and file for free on the app by 2/18. [See offer details](https://blog.turbotax.intuit.com/max-refund-zachking/#offer-details)
findings.location: Hero promo section near top, under "SWITCH TO TURBOTAX"
findings.reasoning:
Rule R-01 applies whenever TurboTax free is mentioned. This page makes multiple prominent "free" claims for TurboTax: "File for $0 on the app" and "file for free on the app by 2/18." These are clear free-filing offers for TurboTax products, so the trigger is met.

The rule then requires that, right underneath such a TurboTax free mention, the specific eligibility disclosure text be present: "~37% of filers qualify. Simple Form 1040 returns only (no schedules, except for EITC, CTC, student loan interest, and Schedule 1-A)." I scanned the hero area where the free claim appears, the subsequent "All tax forms. $0 in the app." section, and the footer/navigation. Nowhere on the page does the required eligibility disclosure (or any substantively equivalent statement) appear, either directly under the free claim or elsewhere on the page.

Because the disclosure is entirely missing, axis A (requirement met) is false. Axis B is also false because there is no text matching the approved D-01 language. This is therefore an unapproved_violation and should be flagged.

The strongest counter-argument is that the offer could be treated as a time-limited promotional waiver ("$0 on the app" by a certain date) distinct from "TurboTax Free" product eligibility and thus outside the rule’s scope. However, the rule’s trigger is defined broadly as "If TurboTax free is mentioned". Marketing statements like "file for free on the app" for TurboTax clearly communicate that TurboTax is free in these circumstances and thus reasonably fall under the rule. Also, even if it were considered a promotional free offer, the rule does not carve out promotional contexts. Therefore I reject that counter-reading and treat the rule as triggered and violated.

findings.location: shared footer, under "Online Software Products" list
findings.reasoning: Trigger met because the page explicitly mentions the TurboTax Free product via the "Free Edition tax filing" link. R-01 requires that, immediately underneath such a mention, the disclosure "~37% of filers qualify. Simple Form 1040 returns only (no schedules, except for EITC, CTC, student loan interest, and Schedule 1-A)." appear. No such disclosure appears underneath that link (or elsewhere on the page). Therefore Axis A is not compliant. Since the required disclosure text is absent, it also does not match the approved library entry, so Axis B is false. Strongest counter-reading: the primary free claim here is a time-limited mobile-app switcher promo (“File for $0 on the app … All tax forms”), which is not the Free Edition; and the footer link is navigational, not promotional. However, the rule, as written, applies whenever TurboTax Free is mentioned, without exempting navigational contexts, so the trigger still applies.
